desc: Aberystwyth is a proper Mid Wales seaside town rather than a resort strip: a university base with a long Victorian promenade, a national cultural archive above the bay, steam-rail access into the Rheidol Valley, and weather that can swing from golden Cardigan Bay evenings to horizontal rain in the same trip. It works best for rail-based slow travel, coastal walking and indoor-outdoor planning rather than guaranteed beach heat.

todo.name: Seafront promenade
todo.desc: Walk the full curve from harbour to Constitution Hill, timing it for sunset over Cardigan Bay.

todo.name: Cliff Railway and camera obscura
todo.desc: Ride up Constitution Hill for coast views when the weather is clear.

todo.name: National Library of Wales
todo.desc: Use the library and exhibitions as Aberystwyth's strongest indoor cultural anchor.

todo.name: Vale of Rheidol Railway
todo.desc: Take the narrow-gauge railway inland to Devil's Bridge for a classic wet-weather-friendly day trip.

todo.name: Aberystwyth Castle and Old College
todo.desc: Link the ruined castle, sea-facing Old College and south promenade to understand the town's university and maritime layers.

todo.name: Ceredigion Coast Path
todo.desc: Use short sections toward Clarach, Borth or Llanrhystud for cliffs, beaches and dolphin-watching chances without committing to a full-distance hike.

wifi.note: Good enough for most town-centre stays and university-area cafes; coverage drops on coast-path sections, valley rail stops and rural guesthouse lanes.

prac.fltNote: Aberystwyth has no airport and no practical nonstop-flight story from LGW/LCY; most visitors arrive by rail via Shrewsbury/Birmingham or by road from Cardiff, Birmingham or Manchester. Accommodation pressure is driven more by UK school holidays, university events, graduation periods and sunny-weekend rail demand than airfare seasonality.
prac.lang: Welsh and English; bilingual signage is routine and Welsh is especially visible in cultural and university contexts
prac.visa: UK domestic destination for British citizens; Irish and other travellers should verify UK entry rules if applicable.
alerts: Cardigan Bay weather changes fast; keep waterproof layers handy even in the better summer months.
Rail journeys are scenic but slow and can be affected by engineering work, so avoid tight same-day airport connections.
Graduation, university open days and summer weekends can make small-town accommodation unexpectedly tight.
The National Library sits above town on Penglais Hill; allow time or use local transport if mobility or rain is a factor.

sweet: Late May to early July is the best balance for long daylight, open attractions and manageable crowds; September is a good quieter backup if you are comfortable with more changeable weather.
